steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: GTM is the air traffic controller. Every flight (DNS query) gets directed to the runway with the shortest queue, best weather, and a working ILS.

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
GTM is health-checked, weighted, geo-aware DNS steering. Properties define data centers (origins) and rules:

- Performance: send users to the lowest-latency DC
- Geographic: route by user country
- Weighted: split traffic by ratio
- Failover: passive secondary kicks in only on health-check failure

Health checks run from Akamai's liveness network — you can't spoof them from origin.

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: Multi-region active-active setup: two LKE clusters (us-east, eu-west), each with a NodeBalancer. GTM Performance property steers each user to their closest cluster. If us-east health-check fails, traffic shifts to eu-west until it recovers.
"GTM = DNS-based traffic steering with health checks. Active-active across regions becomes a config change, not a project."

quiz.question: You want users in Europe to hit your eu-west cluster, US users to hit us-east. What do you configure?

options.explain: NodeBalancers are regional L4/L7 — they don't do geo steering.

options.text: A GTM Geographic property mapping countries to data centers
options.explain: GTM Geographic maps user country/region to a specific data center. DNS resolves to the right NodeBalancer IP.

options.text: EdgeWorker geo redirect on every request
options.explain: Works but adds a redirect hop — GTM does it at DNS resolution, no redirect needed.

options.text: Two separate domains, one per region

quiz.question: How does GTM know your origin is healthy?

options.text: Active liveness checks from Akamai's health-check network
options.explain: GTM probes your origins from multiple Akamai locations. Independent, can't be spoofed from origin.

options.text: Manual ping by your ops team
options.explain: Not scalable. GTM does this automatically.
Failover is automatic on health-check failure.
